coredata.dc:title: Self-polymerization-driven high-purity hydrophobic carbon dots incorporated monolithic columns for high-selectivity reversed-phase capillary liquid chromatography

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) hold great potential for chromatographic separations. However, their application in chromatographic stationary phases has been limited by two major synthetic challenges: (1) insufficient control over surface chemoselectivity caused by impurity co-adsorption, and (2) structural heterogeneity arising from uncontrolled solvothermal byproducts. To address these limitations, we developed a self-polymerization-driven strategy to synthesize high-purity hydrophobic CDs from 8-amino-2-naphthol (8N2OH) and 2,3-dihydroxynaphthalene (DHN). These CDs were incorporated into organic polymer monoliths via in situ copolymerization, yielding novel 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs hybrid monolithic stationary phases for reversed-phase capillary liquid chromatography. The synthesized CDs were thoroughly characterized, with high purity confirmed by proton nuclear magnetic resonance. The resulting monolithic columns exhibited high permeability and uniformity, as demonstrated by field emission scanning electron microscopy, attributed to the excellent dispersion of the CDs. The chromatographic performance was evaluated using nine types of neutral, alkaline, and acidic compounds, as well as Tanaka standard test mixtures. Compared to unmodified columns, the CD-modified monoliths showed significantly enhanced retention strength and selectivity, supported by Tanaka tests revealing multifactorial retention mechanisms. This work demonstrates the potential of high-purity hydrophobic CDs as versatile modifiers for high-performance chromatographic stationary phases.

head.ce:title: Self-polymerization-driven high-purity hydrophobic carbon dots incorporated monolithic columns for high-selectivity reversed-phase capillary liquid chromatography

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) hold great potential for chromatographic separations. However, their application in chromatographic stationary phases has been limited by two major synthetic challenges: (1) insufficient control over surface chemoselectivity caused by impurity co-adsorption, and (2) structural heterogeneity arising from uncontrolled solvothermal byproducts. To address these limitations, we developed a self-polymerization-driven strategy to synthesize high-purity hydrophobic CDs from 8-amino-2-naphthol (8N2OH) and 2,3-dihydroxynaphthalene (DHN). These CDs were incorporated into organic polymer monoliths via in situ copolymerization, yielding novel 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs hybrid monolithic stationary phases for reversed-phase capillary liquid chromatography. The synthesized CDs were thoroughly characterized, with high purity confirmed by proton nuclear magnetic resonance. The resulting monolithic columns exhibited high permeability and uniformity, as demonstrated by field emission scanning electron microscopy, attributed to the excellent dispersion of the CDs. The chromatographic performance was evaluated using nine types of neutral, alkaline, and acidic compounds, as well as Tanaka standard test mixtures. Compared to unmodified columns, the CD-modified monoliths showed significantly enhanced retention strength and selectivity, supported by Tanaka tests revealing multifactorial retention mechanisms. This work demonstrates the potential of high-purity hydrophobic CDs as versatile modifiers for high-performance chromatographic stationary phases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging class of nanomaterials with sizes less than 10 nm, have garnered widespread attention across a wide range of fields due to their abundant and cost-effective carbon sources, facile synthesis processes, good biocompatibility, and exceptional luminescent properties [1–3]. Compared to other carbon nanomaterials, CDs exhibit notable advantages in terms of high specific surface area, excellent solvent dispersibility, moderate adsorption ability, and versatile functional characteristics [4–6]. These characteristics make CDs particularly suitable for constructing chromatographic stationary phases and have shown broad application prospects in high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7–11], gas chromatography (GC) [12], and open tubular capillary electrochromatography (OT-CEC) [13–15].

CDs are typically synthesized via two fundamental approaches: top-down and bottom-up methods. The top-down strategy involves fragmenting bulk carbon materials into nanoscale particles through physical or chemical means, while the bottom-up approach assembles CDs from small organic molecular precursors via controlled carbonization processes. The bottom-up method is generally preferred due to its superior multifunctionality and practical advantages [16]. However, high-temperature treatment of various precursors can lead to uncontrolled complex formation processes and multiple reaction pathways, resulting in the formation of numerous undesired fluorescent byproducts [3,17]. These byproducts may assemble into supramolecular clusters and coexist with the CDs, potentially leading to misleading and inconsistent conclusions about the structure of the studied CDs, as well as significantly impairing their separation performance. Notably, previous studies on CDs-based chromatographic stationary phases have largely overlooked purity verification [18–21], this omission introduces three critical limitations: (1) irreproducible retention behaviors due to batch-to-batch variations, (2) reduced column efficiency from non-uniform surface modification, and (3) compromised separation selectivity caused by impurity-mediated competitive interactions. Therefore, the exploration of efficient methods for synthesizing high-purity CDs and their application as high-performance chromatographic stationary phases are challenging yet crucial.

In addition to packed columns commonly used in HPLC and open tubular columns widely used in GC and CEC, monolithic columns represent another important form of chromatographic stationary phases [22–25]. Their attractive properties, such as excellent permeability, rapid mass transfer, ease of fabrication, and good chemical and mechanical stability, have led to their widespread application across various separation technologies, including HPLC, CEC, solid-phase microextraction, and microfluidic analysis [26–30]. Compared to silica-modified inorganic monoliths, organic polymer monolithic columns exhibit superior pH tolerance and are more amenable to functional modification. These properties make them increasingly popular in the development of various functionalized chromatographic stationary phases [31–34]. Nevertheless, pure polymer monoliths often demonstrate limited selectivity for separating small molecular analytes due to insufficient interaction sites and short retention times [35]. To improve separation performance, many functionalized nanomaterials with high specific surface areas have been incorporated into polymer monoliths, such as metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) [36–38], covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [39,40], and graphene oxide (GO) [41]. However, most MOFs suffer from poor chemical stability, while the synthesis of COFs is typically complex and costly. In contrast, CDs offer distinct advantages as carbon nanomaterials, including facile preparation, robust chemical stability, and excellent solvent compatibility [7,21]. These attributes enable CDs to serve as promising candidates for stable and homogeneous stationary phase modifications, effectively addressing the limitations of existing materials. Despite these advantages, there have been no reports on the preparation and application of CDs-modified monolithic columns as chromatographic stationary phases.

While our earlier work demonstrated the feasibility of self-polymerization for synthesizing hydrophilic polydopamine-derived CDs [42], this approach had not yet been applied to hydrophobic systems. Here, we address these gaps by introducing a self-polymerization-driven strategy to synthesize high-purity hydrophobic CDs derived from 8N2OH and DHN. This method eliminates fluorescent byproducts and ensures structural homogeneity, as rigorously confirmed by 1H NMR. Furthermore, we pioneer the incorporation of CDs into polymeric monoliths via covalent bonding (Fig. 1 ). The successful synthesis and high purity of the 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs, as well as their uniform covalent assembly into the highly permeable polymeric monoliths, were systematically confirmed using proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), and other characterization techniques. Significantly enhanced hydrophobic chromatographic selectivity and baseline separation were achieved on the CDs-modified monolithic columns for all analytes, including alkylbenzenes, methylbenzenes, substituted benzenes, halobenzenes, chlorobenzenes, hydroxybenzoates, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), aromatic amines and phenolic compounds. In addition to demonstrating a wide range of strong separation capabilities, the developed columns also exhibited excellent reproducibility and mechanical stability.

Ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EDMA), glycidyl methacrylate (GMA), 2,2-azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN), 3-methacryloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (γ-MAPS), chlorobenzene, bromobenzene, fluorobenzene, o-triphenyl, styrene, phenol, caffeine, hydroquinone, 4-nitrophenol, and naphthalene were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Benzene, methylbenzene, ethylbenzene, trimethylbenzene, allylbenzene, n-propylbenzene, n-butylbenzene, n-amylbenzene and dimethylbenzene were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from KeLong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). 1,4-butanediol (1,4-BDO), n-propanol (NPA), absolute ethanol (EtOH), aniline, 1-naphthylamine, o-phenylenediamine, and N,N-dimethylaniline were acquired from Jizhi Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methyl 4-hydroxybenzoate, ethyl 4-hydroxybenzoate, propyl 4-hydroxybenzoate, butyl 4-hydroxybenzoate and fluorene were obtained from Dibio Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 8N2OH, DHN, potassium iodate (KIO3), and sodium periodate (NaIO4) were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HPLC-grade acetonitrile (ACN) and methanol (MeOH) were obtained from Hongben Chemical Products Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Ultrapure water was produced using an AWL-1002-H water purification system (Aikepu, Chongqing, China). Fused-silica capillary (100 μm i.d. × 375 μm o.d.) was from Ruifeng Chromatographic Devices Co., Ltd. (Hebei, China).

All chromatographic separation experiments were conducted using a TriSep-3000 pressurized CLC system equipped with a UV detector (Unimicro Technologies, Pleasanton, CA, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy analysis was performed using an IRAffinity-1S spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The morphological observations of the capillary inner surface were performed using a SU8020 FESEM (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with an EMAX mics2 energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS, HORIBA, Japan). Water contact angle (WCA) measurements were conducted using an FCA2000A contact angle meter (Aifeisi, Shanghai, China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM-1200EX electron microscope operating at an accelerating voltage of 120 kV, while high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were acquired using a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin electron microscope. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CDs samples were analyzed using a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer and Perkin-Elmer luminescence spectrometer (LS-55) respectively. The elemental component analysis was conducted using an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Electron, USA). The 1H NMR spectra were recorded using a Bruker AV III 400 spectrometer operating at 400 MHz.

First, 100 mg of KIO3, 10 mL of ultrapure water (H2O), and 40 mL of EtOH were added to a Teflon-lined reactor. After thorough mixing and stirring, 50 mg of 8N2OH was introduced into the mixture. The reaction proceeded at 220 °C for 12 h. Upon completion, the mixture was cooled to room temperature and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The resulting crude CD solution was then added to 1 L of ultrapure water to form an insoluble precipitate [43]. The residual reactants and solvent in the supernatant were then washed away. Finally, the purified precipitate containing 8N2OH-CDs were obtained via vacuum filtration and freeze-drying. Similarly, DHN-CDs were synthesized using the same protocol, with only the precursor type being altered.

Prior to polymerization, a newly purchased fused silica capillary with a total length of 60.0 cm was sequentially flushed with 1 M NaOH (2 h), H2O (30 min), 0.1 M HCl (1 h), and H2O (30 min), followed by a 30 min rinse with MeOH. The activated capillary was then dried under a nitrogen stream for 1 h. Subsequently, a 50 % (v/v) γ-MAPS MeOH solution was injected into the capillary, which was sealed on both ends and placed in a 60 °C water bath for 12 h. After the reaction, the capillary was rinsed with MeOH and nitrogen for 30 min each.

Next, the polymerization solution was formulated based on existing literature [44], consisting of 15 wt% GMA as the monomers, 15 wt% EDMA as the crosslinking agent, and varying amounts of 8N2OH-CDs (0.5, 1, 2 mg/mL) as functional modifiers. These components were dissolved in a 70 wt% binary porogen system consisting of 35 wt% 1,4-BDO and 35 wt% NPA. After adding 1.5 mg of AIBN as the initiator, the mixture was vortexed to achieve homogeneity and then sonicated for 25 min to eliminate air bubbles. The resulting polymerization solutions (approximately 1 mL) were then infused into the pre-treated capillaries to a length of 35.0 cm. The ends of the columns were immediately sealed with rubber stoppers and immersed in a constant-temperature water bath at 50 °C, 60 °C, and 70 °C for 12 h. Upon completion of the polymerization, approximately 2.0 cm from the inlet segment of the columns was cut off, and the columns were thoroughly washed with MeOH using a syringe pump until all unreacted residuals were eliminated, followed by nitrogen drying to yield the 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-co-EDMA) monoliths (Fig. 1). Utilizing a similar methodology, the DHN-CDs-incorporated monoliths were synthesized, denoted as DHN-CDs-poly(GMA-co-EDMA), while monoliths polymerized without CDs were designated as poly(GMA-co-EDMA).

At approximately 0.5 cm from the edge of the stationary phases, a detection window measuring 3 mm was created by burning off the outer coating of the prepared columns. The total length of the fabricated columns was 58.0 cm, with an effective length of 33.5 cm. Additionally, the monolithic skeletons were fabricated in centrifuge tubes under identical polymerization formulations and reaction conditions, followed by repeated MeOH washing to remove unreacted residues, and vacuum drying at 60 °C.

A standard stock solution of analytes (5.0 mg/mL) was prepared using ACN and stored under refrigeration at 4 °C. The mobile phase comprised H2O and an appropriate ratio of ACN, which was subsequently degassed via ultrasonication prior to use. The injected samples were prepared by diluting the standard solutions with the mobile phase. Prior to experimentation, all solutions were filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane filter. Following daily CLC operations, the 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs-integrated monoliths were rinsed with MeOH for 30 min and stored at 25 °C. The pump flow rate was maintained at 0.05 mL/min, with a split ratio of approximately 40:1. An injection volume of 2 µL was used before splitting, yielding an actual injected volume of 50 nL.

As evidenced by our previous study, dopamine and its phenolic analogues can be employed as self-polymerizable precursors for the efficient synthesis of high-purity hydrophilic CDs through a self-polymerization driven bottom-up growth process. A critical challenge that remains is the development of a simple and high-yield method for producing high-purity hydrophobic CDs [4]. In this study, we hypothesized that 8N2OH and DHN, two commonly available and inexpensive hydrophobic phenolic compounds, could also be effectively converted into hydrophobic CDs via a self-driven bottom-up reaction route. To verify this hypothesis, various representative reaction systems were utilized for the solvothermal synthesis of 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs, including EtOH, Tris buffer/EtOH (20:80, v/v), NaIO4 aqueous solution/EtOH (20:80, v/v) [45,46], and KIO3 aqueous solution/EtOH (20:80, v/v) [47]. As illustrated in Fig. S1, when using conventional EtOH or Tris/EtOH reaction systems, the purified yields of solvothermal products derived from 8N2OH and DHN precursors were consistently low (all below 15.0 %). The observed low conversion efficiency is consistent with prior reports, and can be attributed to the massive formation of undesired byproducts alongside the CDs, resulting from uncontrolled complex bottom-up formation processes and diverse solvothermal reaction pathways [42,45]. By contrast, after the introduction of chemical oxidation conditions (NaIO4 or KIO3) to induce self-polymerization, the yields of the highly purified retentates showed varying degrees of improvement. This phenomenon confirms the effectiveness of the self-polymerization strategy in enhancing the formation efficiency of CDs from the self-polymerizable hydrophobic precursors. Notably, the highest yields were achieved in the KIO3/EtOH-mediated reaction system​(27.2 % for 8N2OH-CDs and 37.3 % for DHN-CDs), attributable to the moderate oxidation capacity of KIO3, which promotes controllable polymerization and efficient formation of 8N2OH-CDs and minimizes the formation of insoluble solvothermal char (Fig. S1) and low-molecular-weight byproducts during solvothermal processing. Therefore, the KIO3/EtOH system was selected as the optimal reaction medium for subsequent synthesis and characterization of 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs.

To confirm the successful synthesis of 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs mediated by KIO3/EtOH, the morphologies of the purified products were analyzed using TEM (Fig. 2 A and Fig. S2A). Both samples exhibited a significant quantity of well-defined quasi-spherical nanoparticles with average sizes of approximately 3.5 nm and 2.3 nm, respectively (Fig. 2B and Fig. S2B). Notably, no significant signs of molecular or particle aggregation were observed in the TEM images, preliminarily confirming the high purity and good dispersibility of the prepared CDs. HR-TEM images further indicated that these nanoparticles are crystalline, with a measured lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 2A and Fig. S2A), corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite [48]. The surface chemical composition of the two types of CDs was characterized using FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2G, the characteristic absorption band at 3420 cm−1 for 8N2OH-CDs is attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H groups, while the peak at 3165 cm−1 corresponds to N-H stretching vibrations. For DHN-CDs, the infrared absorption band at 3438 cm−1 similarly indicates O-H group vibrations (Fig. 2H). Additionally, their common absorbance bands around 1600 cm−1 suggest the presence of a benzene skeleton structure on the surfaces of both 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs.

Further insights into the functional groups and elemental composition of the synthesized CDs were obtained through XPS analysis. The observed full spectrum (Fig. 2D–F and S2D–F) indicates that both 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs possess a high carbon content, confirming the effective graphitization of the solvothermal products. High proportions of C-O, and N-H bonds were identified in the high-resolution C1s and N1s spectra of 8N2OH-CDs, indicating the presence of hydroxyl and amino groups on their surface. Besides, the existence of hydroxyl groups on the surface of DHN-CDs was revealed by their high-resolution C1s and O1s spectra. The XPS results are consistent with the findings of the FT-IR analysis, collectively indicating that both types of CDs are highly carbonized and composed of graphite microstructures. Notably, the amino and hydroxyl groups bonded to the surface of 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs provide essential reactive sites for their subsequent covalent assembly into the monolithic matrixes via epoxy ring-opening reactions.

Both CDs exhibited characteristic absorption peaks at 230 nm in their UV–vis absorption spectra. These absorption features can be attributed to the π–π* electronic transition behavior in aromatic conjugated systems, indicating the presence of effective conjugated structures within the materials. This conclusion is consistent with the FT-IR analysis results.Furthermore, the CDs demonstrated strong and highly stable fluorescence emission under varying conditions (Fig. S3C–H), with measured quantum yields of 3.8 % for 8N2OH-CDs and 1.8 % for DHN-CDs. The good fluorescence stability suggests excellent structural integrity, ensuring reliable long-term performance in subsequent chromatographic applications.

Next, 1H NMR analysis was employed to assess the purity of the as-prepared CDs, a highly sensitive and indispensable technique for identifying potential molecular impurities in CDs samples. Due to the low hydrogen content, complex structure, and high heterogeneity of CDs, a pure CDs sample should display only broad and unresolved peaks in the 1H NMR spectrum, in contrast to molecular impurities, which typically manifest as intense and sharp signals. As illustrated in Fig. 2I–J, the unpurified 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs samples reveal sharp and strong signals in the aliphatic region (1–4 ppm) of their 1H NMR spectra, indicating the presence of various residual molecular byproducts. Following rigorous purification by water-wash method, the original sharp signals disappear completely in the spectra of the purified products, leaving only broad and unresolved peaks. These phenomena are consistent with previous studies [42,49–51] and provide compelling evidence for confirming the high purity of the thoroughly purified 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs samples.

The WCA measurement was utilized to characterize the surface wetting behavior of these two highly purified CDs samples. As illustrated in Fig. 2C, the 8N2OH-CDs exhibited a high degree of hydrophobicity with a WCA of 131.28 ± 0.5°. By contrast, DHN-CDs displayed slightly weaker hydrophobicity (Fig. S2C), with a WCA value of 121.89 ± 0.7°. The observed strong hydrophobicity of 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs can be attributed to several factors: (i) the intrinsic aromatic structures of the precursors facilitates the formation of abundant hydrophobic graphite microstructures within the CDs; (ii) the hydrophilic hydroxyl and amino groups of 8N2OH and DHN were largely converted into hydrophobic carbonyl and pyrrole groups during the solvothermal process, as confirmed by XPS analysis; (iii) the high proportion of EtOH (80 %, v/v) in the reaction solvent can provide a hydrophobic microenvironment throughout the reaction, which is conducive to preserving the hydrophobic characteristics of the final products. Benefiting from the strong hydrophobicity of 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs, both can maintain excellent long-term dispersibility in a mixed solvent composed of equal volumes of 1,4-BDO and NPA (Fig. S4), which provides essential prerequisites for the subsequent preparation of CDs-incorporated polymer monoliths.

The fundamental components and fabrication processes for CDs-incorporated monolithic columns were based on a previously reported, well-optimized, and generic poly(GMA-co-EDMA) precursor monolith, which contribute to minimizing the time-consuming and complex trial-and-error phases in monolith preparation [52,53]. After further confirming the good dispersion of the synthesized CDs with the polymerization solution (Fig. S6), we systematically evaluated the effects of reaction temperature and CDs concentration on the permeability and separation efficiency of the resultant monolith. As detailed in Table S1, considering the balance between good permeability and high column efficiency, the polymerization temperature was determined to be 60 °C, and the optimal concentration of 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs was 1 mg/mL.

The inner surface morphologies of poly(GMA-co-EDMA), 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-co-EDMA), and DHN-CDs-poly(GMA-co-EDMA) monolithic columns were characterized using FESEM. As shown in Fig. 3 A-B, the poly(GMA-co-EDMA) column is filled with a highly homogeneous and porous monolithic skeleton that tightly adheres to the capillary inner wall. Although the incorporation of 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs into the polymerization system resulted in localized aggregation of the polymer nanoparticles and a slight decrease in permeability (Table S1), the overall uniformity distribution and strong adhesion of the resulting polymeric matrix to the tube wall were well maintained.

The areal distribution and density of C, O and N elements on the three monolithic columns were thoroughly investigated using EDS analysis. The results indicated that both 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-co-EDMA) and DHN-CDs-poly(GMA-co-EDMA) exhibited stronger signals for C and O elements compared to poly(GMA-co-EDMA) (Fig. 3C–D and S5B). Additionally, a new nitrogen elemental signal was observed in the 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-co-EDMA) sample. These findings collectively demonstrate that 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs were effectively incorporated into the monolithic skeleton. Moreover, the uniform distribution of the C element in the two CDs-incorporated monoliths further confirms that 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs were uniformly doped into the polymeric matrix.

The functional groups of the prepared monoliths were further characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2G–H, poly(GMA-co-EDMA) exhibits characteristic absorption bands associated with the stretching vibrations of CO and C–O–C groups at approximately 1729 cm−1 and 1150 cm−1, indicating the successful occurrence of radical polymerization reaction. After the introduction of the two types of CDs, these characteristic absorption bands of the polymeric matrix were well maintained, while new absorption peaks related to 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs appeared, further validating their successful incorporation. Additionally, the characteristic peaks associated with hydroxyl and amine groups showed a slight shift, confirming the occurrence of a ring-opening reaction between these CDs and the vinyl monomers. These findings are consistent with the EDS characterization results, collectively demonstrating the uniform cross-linking and incorporation of 8N2OH-CDs and DHN-CDs within the monolithic skeleton.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of operation conditions on the separation performance of monoliths
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of flow rate and ACN concentration on the separation performance of 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA) for alkylbenzenes was thoroughly investigated. As illustrated in Figs. S7 and S8, to achieve a balance between optimum selectivity, high column efficiency, and moderate analysis time, the optimal operation conditions were determined to be a flow rate of 0.05 mL/min and an ACN concentration of 45 % (v/v) for subsequent CLC separation experiments. Under these conditions, all five alkylbenzenes were rapidly baseline separated within 16 min, with a theoretical plate number as high as 10,437 plates/m (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of separation performance of CDs-incorporated polymer monoliths
Evaluation of separation mechanism of CDs-incorporated polymer monoliths
ce:section.ce:para: To reveal the chromatographic retention behavior of the 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA) column, the retention patterns of three model analytes, i.e., aromatic amines, chlorobenzenes and halogenated benzenes, were investigated on 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA) by changing the ACN content. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A–C, the retention of all three compounds gradually decreased with increasing ACN concentration, demonstrating a typical reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) retention mechanism. In RPLC, it is well-established that the retention factor (k) for a homologous series increases exponentially with the number of homologous units [60]. Fig. 5H presents plots of the number of methylene groups in alkylbenzenes versus the logarithm ofk(log k). Strong linear relationships were observed between the number of methylene groups and log k values across different ACN proportions in the mobile phase, with correlation coefficients reaching 0.99. These results further confirm the excellent hydrophobic selectivity of the 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA) stationary phase, which is attributed to the strong hydrophobicity of the 8N2OH-CDs integrated into the monolithic columns.

To further elucidate the separation mechanism of the 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA) column, a Tanaka standard test was conducted [61,62]. This test is widely used to evaluate hydrophobic selectivity, hydrogen bonding capacity, and other properties of liquid chromatography stationary phases [63,64]. The retention factor for n-pentylbenzene (k PB), which reflects the surface coverage and area of the monolithic stationary phase, was significantly higher at 1.269 for 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA) compared to poly(GMA-EDMA) and DNH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA), as detailed in Table 2 . Additionally, the significantly higher separation factor (αCH2 = 1.328) for n-pentylbenzene and n-butylbenzene on the 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA) column confirms enhanced hydrophobic selectivity of the stationary phase. On the other hand, the retention factor ratio of n-pentylbenzene and o-triphenyl (αPB/O = kPB/kO) on 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA) and DNH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA) was slightly lower than that of poly(GMA-EDMA).This phenomenon was attributed to the significantly prolonged retention time of o-terphenyl, which has a higher number of aromatic rings, on both CDs-modified phases. Given the abundant graphitic structures of 8N2OH-CDs and DNH-CDs, it is evident that aromatic selectivity, driven by π-π interactions between the incorporated CDs and aromatic compounds, also contributes to their enhanced separation selectivity. The slightly enhanced hydrogen bonding interaction of 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA) and DNH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA) compared to poly(GMA-EDMA) was also demonstrated by their higher retention factor ratio for caffeine and phenol (αC/P = kC/kP). Collectively, the observed superior separation selectivity of 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA) and DNH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA) can be attributed not only to strong hydrophobic interactions but also to the potential contributions of π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding.

To assess the repeatability of the prepared 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA) stationary phase, we determined the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the retention times for alkylbenzenes (Table S4 and Fig. 5D–F). Notably, the corresponding intra-day, inter-day RSD were found to be below 2.3 % on these two columns. The RSD in retention times for the monolithic columns prepared from three independent batches ranged from 6.86 % to 7.28 % (n = 3). Furthermore, following 200 consecutive runs, the separation efficiency for alkylbenzenes remained constant on 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA) monolith (Fig. 5G), without any notable deviation in retention time. These findings demonstrate the exceptional repeatability and long-term stability of 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA) monolith columns developed in this study.

Next, in order to evaluate the mechanical stability of the prepared monolithic column, the backpressure of the column was systematically tested by pumping distinct mobile phases (100 % ACN, 100 % H2O, and 55:45 ACN/H2O mixture) at different flow rates. As shown in Fig. 5I, a good mechanical stability is confirmed by the excellent linearity relationship between backpressure and flow rate over the range of 0.02 to 0.06 mL/min. Specifically, across all tested mobile phase conditions, a linear relationship was observed between column pressure and flow rate, with coefficient of determination (R2) values exceeding 0.999 for all three mobile phases. This result further demonstrates the superior mechanical stability of the 8N2OH-CDs-poly(GMA-EDMA).

In conclusion, we present a self-polymerization-driven synthesis of high-purity hydrophobic CDs, addressing the critical purity issue through rigorous 1H NMR validation. By covalently embedding these CDs into polymeric monoliths, we develop the first CDs-based monolithic stationary phases for capillary liquid chromatography. The 8N2OH-CDs column exhibits superior separation performance, driven by enhanced hydrophobic interactions, π-π stacking, and hydrogen bonding, achieving rapid baseline separation of diverse analytes with excellent reproducibility. This work not only provides a scalable route to high-purity CDs but also establishes their transformative potential in chromatographic science, bridging the gap between nanomaterial innovation and practical separation technologies.
